straight_beat.name: Delmar Fontenot
straight_beat.column:
The Astros lost yesterday, but not without a fight. Seattle’s 8-7 win over Houston was a reminder that even in a division as competitive as the AL West, one misstep can cost you a game. The script was familiar: Houston’s offense showed flashes of life, the pitching staff kept things close, and then—poof—the Mariners’ bats found a groove. It’s the kind of game that makes you wonder if the team’s DNA is finally catching up to its potential.  

Let’s talk about the pitching. Framber Valdez was solid, six innings and a third, allowing three runs. That’s the tape measure of it. But here’s where the Astros’ rotation starts to unravel. The starters are throwing enough innings to make a manager proud, but their ERAs are creeping toward the .500 mark. Valdez is a workhorse, sure, but the other four? They’re averaging 5.2 innings per start, which isn’t bad, but when you’re a team that’s supposed to be a playoff contender, you need more than just quantity. You need quality. The bullpen, meanwhile, remains a question mark. The closer’s role has shifted so often this season it’s hard to keep track, and the setup men are still figuring out how to bridge the gap between the starter and the late innings.  

Then there’s the offense. Altuve singled in the fifth, Alvarez went yard in the seventh, and Valdez went six and a third. Houston wins, 5-2. That’s the script from a few weeks ago. The problem is, it’s not consistent. The Astros have the pieces to score runs

optimist.name: Luz Barquero
optimist.column:
The Astros lost yesterday, but let’s not let that dampen our spirits. Losing a game is like a rain delay—temporary, inevitable, and often a setup for something bigger. The boys in the dugout? They’re not just playing to win; they’re playing to prove that a 1.5-game gap in the AL West is just a blip on the radar. Bregman’s swing, that steady as heck swing, is one at-bat away from flipping April into June. You hear that? The calendar’s already turning, and the team’s got the momentum to keep it spinning.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: “Luz, they’re 6-9. How’s that ‘cookin’?” Let me tell you, this team’s got the heart of a man who’s been through a few oil changes. My husband’s been glued to the TV for weeks, and not because he’s a fan of the drama. No, he’s there for the grit. The way the Astros keep showing up, even when the odds are stacked. Last night’s game? Sure, they dropped the ball, but you could see the fire in their eyes. That’s the kind of fight that turns losses into lessons.  

Let’s talk about the bullpen. Those guys? They’re the unsung heroes, and they’ve got the chops to keep the team in games. You can’t teach a pitcher to throw a shutout, but you can teach them to stay calm under pressure. And stay calm they did, even when the Mariners were clawing their way back. The Astros’ defense? That’s like a well-tuned tire shop—tight, reliable, and always ready to keep things rolling.  

Now, I’ll be the first to admit: this team’s got some growing pains. They’re not perfect, and that’s okay. The league’s tough, but so is H-town

pessimist.name: Coop Yarbrough
pessimist.column:
The Astros lost again. Not in a way that’s easy to explain, either. They were up, they were down, they were up again, and then the Mariners’ bullpen—because why not—found a way to make it all matter. Seven runs. Eight. A game that should have been a coronation, and instead it’s another chapter in the same old story. Rigged from the jump.  

The offense, for all its talk about “clutch” and “resilience,” couldn’t string a clean hit together. Every time they looked like they might turn the page, someone—somebody—would hit a ground ball to the shortstop, or a fly ball to the wall, and
